steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.impendere: 'impendere' is an infinitive meaning 'to hang over / threaten'

hints.to confide in: Close, but 'confidere' in Latin means 'to trust firmly / be confident that'

hints.Perfect indicative: 'confido' is semi-deponent: perfect would be 'confisus sum' — 'confido' is present
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'confidam' — 'confido' is indicative
hints.Future indicative: Future would be 'confidam' (same form as subjunctive) — 'confido' is present indicative

hints.fido, fidere, fisus sum: These are the parts of 'fidere' (to trust) — the base verb without con- prefix
hints.confundo, confundere, confudi, confusum: These are the parts of 'confundere' (to confuse/pour together)
hints.confirmo, confirmare, confirmavi, confir: These are the parts of 'confirmare' (to strengthen)

hints.Nominative: Nominative plural would be 'qui/quae' — 'Quibus' is dative/ablative; here dative with 'impendere'
hints.Ablative: 'Quibus' can be ablative, but with 'impendere' (to hang over) it is dative — threat hangs over them

hints.Nominative: Neuter nom. and acc. are the same form, but 'fatum' is the subject-accusative of the infinitive 'impendere'
hints.Genitive: Genitive would be 'fati' — 'fatum' is accusative in indirect statement

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'fatum, fati' is 2nd declension neuter
hints.3rd: 3rd declension neuters have varied endings — 'fatum, fati' is 2nd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns have gen. in -us — 'fatum, fati' has gen. -i (2nd)

hints.Ut + subjunctive: 'confidere' can take 'ut' clause, but here 'fatum … impendere' is accusative + infinitive
hints.Quod + indicative: 'quod' clauses can follow verbs of emotion, but 'confido' here takes acc. + inf.
hints.Dative + infinitive: There is no standard dative + infinitive construction — 'confido' takes acc. + inf.

translation: Over whom I am confident some doom hangs.
